coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe constructed of water-soluble dual-element-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and highly sensitive detection of Ag+
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Wenya
Yan, Jiaqi
Wu, Qingsheng
Hu, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: Ag+, extensively used in our daily life, has caused serious harm to human health and ecological environment, so highly selective detection and accurate quantification of Ag+ are critical. Herein, we report a novel water-soluble dual-element composite doped carbon quantum dots, N,S-CDs, then constructed them into fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection. Carbon source ammonium citrate (AC) could achieve nitrogen doping without additional nitrogen source, inorganic sulfur source sodium thiosulfate was used for rapid sulfur doping, the N,S-CDs emitting dazzling blue fluorescence were obtained through a rapid hydrothermal process. The synthesized N,S-CDs had an average particle size of 3.25 nm, with high quantum yield (QY = 36.8%) and excitation-independent fluorescence properties. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs could detect Ag+ with high sensitivity and fast response, showing a good piecewise linear relationship (0.1–700 μM). In addition, the fluorescent probe exhibited remarkable anti-interference ability and reusability, which is practical and feasible, and has broad application prospects in biological and environmental sample detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-CDs
Fluorescent probe
Ag+
Selective detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFC1808802), the Young and Middle-aged Science and Technology Talent Development Fund (ZQ2020-3) and the Talent Introduction Fund (YJ2021-33) of Shanghai Institute of Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: QY comparison of N,S-CDs synthesized under various reaction conditions: (A) mole ratio of AC to Na2S2O3; (B) temperature; (C) time.
(A) TEM image. (B) Column chart of particle diameter distribution. (C) High-resolution TEM image with lattice fringe annotation as an inset. (D) XRD diagram.
(A) FTIR and (B) full-scan XPS spectra. (C-F) High-resolved XPS spectra of C 1s (C), O 1s (D), N 1s (E) and S 2p (F).

(A) UV–vis (blue), fluorescence excitation (EX, black), fluorescence emission (EM, red) spectra. Insets are photographs of N,S-CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs under various pH (inset is the fluorescence intensity). (B) Relative PL intensity (F0/F) during continuous irradiation for 150 min under 365 nm UV lamp. (C) Relative PL intensity (F0/F) in various concentrations (0–2.0 mol/L) of NaCl solutions. (F0 represents the initial PL intensity, F represents the PL intensity under other conditions.)

(A) The influence of different metal ions (2 mM) on F0/F (inset is the corresponding luminescence photograph). (B) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs in the Ag+ concentration ([Ag+]) range from 0 to 800 μM (inset is the corresponding photograph under 365 nm UV lamp). (C) Piecewise linear relationship of lg(F0/F) to [Ag+]. (D) Relative PL intensity (F0/F) of N,S-CDs (black bars), N,S-CDs + Ag+ (500 μM) (red bars), and N,S-CDs + Ag++Mn+ (other metal ions, 500 μM) (blue bars).
Hydrothermal synthesis process of N,S-CDs and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Ag+.
Performance comparison of N,S-CDs probe and other Ag+ fluorescent probes.
Measurement of [Ag+] in actual tap water (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe constructed of water-soluble dual-element-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and highly sensitive detection of Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ag+, extensively used in our daily life, has caused serious harm to human health and ecological environment, so highly selective detection and accurate quantification of Ag+ are critical. Herein, we report a novel water-soluble dual-element composite doped carbon quantum dots, N,S-CDs, then constructed them into fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection. Carbon source ammonium citrate (AC) could achieve nitrogen doping without additional nitrogen source, inorganic sulfur source sodium thiosulfate was used for rapid sulfur doping, the N,S-CDs emitting dazzling blue fluorescence were obtained through a rapid hydrothermal process. The synthesized N,S-CDs had an average particle size of 3.25 nm, with high quantum yield (QY = 36.8%) and excitation-independent fluorescence properties. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs could detect Ag+ with high sensitivity and fast response, showing a good piecewise linear relationship (0.1–700 μM). In addition, the fluorescent probe exhibited remarkable anti-interference ability and reusability, which is practical and feasible, and has broad application prospects in biological and environmental sample detection.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, pollution caused by various heavy metal ions has evolved into a global environmental problem threatening human health and ecosystems. Silver (Ag), as a precious metal, is widely used in electronics, photography, pharmacy and other industrial fields, which is of great significance to humans [1–3]. Therefore, there is a large amount of waste silver discharged from factories into our environment. Many studies have reported that Ag+ has a strong affinity with amino and sulfhydryl groups, so it can combine with proteins and nucleic acids that are important in our body to form harmful complexes, causing serious harm to human health [4,5]. Therefore, highly selective and quantitative determination of Ag+ is crucial for health and environmental monitoring. The detection methods of heavy metal ions mainly include ultraviolet visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis), electrochemical stripping voltammetry, inductively coupled plasma method, enzyme analysis, atomic absorption spectrometry, etc [6–9]. Although these methods are sensitive and reliable, they also have some disadvantages, such as requiring expensive large-scale equipment, complicated sample preparation and processing, poor selectivity, and inability to achieve rapid on-site detection. Fluorescence detection has received wide attention because it has the characteristics of cheapness, convenient operation, high sensitivity, strong selectivity, intuition, and fast response [10]. The materials currently used for fluorescence detection mainly include organic dyes, metal nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots, and fluorescent carbon nanomaterials.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as emerging fluorescent nanoparticles, have received wide attention since Xu discovered them in 2004 [11]. Compared with other fluorescent materials, CDs have the characteristics of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy functionalization, fluorescence tunability and strong photostability [12,13]. However, CDs usually show a low quantum yield, which hinders their application in actual environmental detection. To improve the performance of CDs, chemical modification can be carried out during the synthesis process. Doping heteroatoms (such as nitrogen or sulfur atoms) in CDs is an effective strategy to adjust their structure and performance [14–16]. The sensitivity of detecting heavy metal ions depends on their fluorescence quenching efficiency, while the functional groups on carbon quantum dots containg N and S can enhance their chelating ability with metal ions, thus resulting in effective fluorescence quenching [17–19]. Using uric acid as a precursor, Wang et al. prepared a kind of N-CDs and realized high sensitivity detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ [20]. Guo et al. successfully prepared N-CDs with rice residue and glycine as raw materials, and fabricated a highly sensitive sensor for Fe3+ detection [21]. Obviously, doping heteroatoms into CDs can effectively improve their optical properties. Therefore, the construction of a simple, highly sensitive, heteroatom-doped CDs-based Ag+ fluorescent probe is highly desirable.

In this work, based on the strong affinity of Ag+ with amino and sulfhydryl groups, a novel water-soluble dual-element composite doped carbon quantum dots, N,S-CDs, was prepared through a simple hydrothermal process, in which ammonium citrate (AC) was the source of C and N, and sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) was the S source. The obtained N,S-CDs showed high QY (36.8%) without further chemical modification, and had excellent optical stability. The fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs had high selectivity and a wide detection range for Ag+, which has broad application prospects in environmental and biological sample detection.

The synthesis process of N,S-CDs is described in Scheme 1 , where AC acted as carbon source and nitrogen source simultaneously, and Na2S2O3 acted as sulfur source. First, AC and Na2S2O3 were heat-decomposed to produce citric acid, NH3, Na2S and so on. Then, in the co-existence of NH3 and S2-, citric acid was carbonized at high temperature to form N,S-CDs nuclei. Finally, under the regulation of –COOH and –OH, the crystal nuclei continued to grow along the surface to form N,S-CDs. The fluorescence QY of N,S-CDs is affected by many factors, including the molar ratio of AC (nitrogen source) to Na2S2O3 (sulfur source) in the reaction solution, temperature and time of the hydrothermal reaction. As can be seen from Fig. 1 A, when the mole ratio of AC to Na2S2O3 was 1.0:1, the quantum yield was the highest, reaching 36.8%. Excessive nitrogen source might block passivation surface defects, resulting in a decrease in QY. Increasing the carbonization temperature, QY increased first and then decreased, and reached the maximum at 160 °C (Fig. 1B). The data in Fig. 1C showed that QY was the highest when the hydrothermal reaction time was 5 h. Therefore, the optimal conditions for synthesizing N,S-CDs were 2.5 mM AC, 2.5 mM Na2S2O3, 160 °C and 5 h.

The microscopic morphology, structure, elemental species and functional groups of the synthesized N,S-CDs were characterized. TEM image clearly displayed that N,S-CDs were spherical, with good dispersion and no agglomeration (Fig. 2 A). The average size was about 3.25 nm, and the particle diameter distribution was narrow, ranging between 1.5 and 5.2 nm (150 particles were randomly chosen and measured), which well conforms to the Gaussian distribution (Fig. 2B). Most of N,S-CDs were amorphous carbon without clear lattice fringes (Fig. 2C). The lattice spacing of a few carbon dots measured from high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was 0.21 nm, similar to the spacing of (100) crystal face in sp2 hybrid carbon materials (Fig. 2C, inset). There was a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.5° in the XRD diagram, indicating that the synthesized material was amorphous and had a graphite-like structure (Fig. 2D).

The functional group types of N,S-CDs were determined by FTIR (Fig. 3 A and Fig. S1). The two adjacent vibration peaks at 3415 and 3185 cm−1 suggested that the structure contained –NH2 and –OH, so N,S-CDs exhibited very good hydrophilicity [22,23]. The strong band generated by the C=C bond of the conjugate structure appeared at 1589 cm−1. The weak peak at 2360 cm−1 represented the S-H bond, and C-N, C-S bonds existed in the 1200–1400 cm−1 interval, all of which suggested that sulfur and nitrogen were successfully doped in N,S-CDs.

The elemental species and composition of N,S-CDs were further analyzed through XPS. N,S-CDs mainly contained four elements, which were carbon (284.3 eV, 60.45%), oxygen (530.9 eV, 26.72%), nitrogen (399.3 eV, 8.59%), and sulfur (163.4 eV, 4.24%) (Fig. 3B). High-resolved XPS spectra further indicated the combination modes of the above four elements. The three peaks of C 1s (284.2, 285.0 and 287.6 eV) represented C-C, C-N/C-S and C=O (Fig. 3C) [24]. The O 1s spectrum suggested the existence of C-O (530.8 eV) and C=O (532.2 eV) (Fig. 3D). The three peaks in N 1s spectrum belonged to pyridinic-N (C=N), pyrrolic-N (C-N) and N-H (Fig. 3E). The four peaks of S 2p spectrum demonstrated the presence of C-S and C-SOx species (Fig. 3F). The results of XPS were consistent with those of FTIR, indicating that AC and Na2S2O3 can be used to synthesize N,S-CDs in a simple and rapid method. Carbon source AC can achieve nitrogen doping without additional nitrogen source, and the operation is simple. At present, the commonly used nitrogen sources, such as ethylene diamine and m-phenylenediamine, are all organic substances, and have a certain irritation, the synthesis method is not environmentally friendly. As an inorganic sulfur source, Na2S2O3 is decomposed at high temperature to generate Na2S, which can quickly release S2-, thereby achieving efficient doping of S element.

The photoluminescence (PL) performance of the prepared N,S-CDs was measured using fluorescence spectrophotometer. There was almost no overlap between the excitation peak (343 nm) and the emission peak (435 nm) in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4 A). The narrow fluorescence emission spectrum suggested the prepared N,S-CDs were homologous and monodisperse. A very weak peak appeared near 236 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, indicating the presence of conjugated double bonds (C=C) in the structure (Fig. S2). There was also a broad peak near 343 nm, corresponding to the fluorescence excitation spectrum, which belonged to the n-π* energy transition. This peak produced a strong fluorescence emission because the energy of the excited state was emitted in the form of photons. In addition, N,S-CDs could emit dazzling fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 4A, inset), suggesting that the material had fewer surface defects, thus inhibiting the non-radiative transition and improving the luminescence efficiency. The spectra in Fig. 4B showed that the PL intensity changed significantly at different excitation wavelengths, but the peak position was barely moved. This excitation-independent fluorescence property is attributed to the uniform particle diameter distribution, which is conducive to quantitative detection [25,26].

Fluorescence stability is an important indicator to evaluate whether the synthesized N,S-CDs can be applied to practical detection. Fig. 5 A showed the PL intensity of N,S-CDs in phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, 0.01 M) with different pH. Under acidic conditions (pH = 3–5), the PL intensity was very weak. When pH increased to 6, the PL intensity increased significantly, and almost no longer increased with the change of pH. This phenomenon suggests that the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs has broad pH applicability. Under strong acidic conditions, protonation occurred at the surface emission site, and the probability of electron radiation transition was reduced, leading to fluorescence weakening or even quenching. When studying the photobleaching performance, the relative PL intensity (F0/F) was not weakened significantly during continuous irradiation for 150 min under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5B and Fig. S3-A). This phenomenon suggests that N,S-CDs overcome the shortcomings of organic fluorescent dyes that are easily photobleached. Moreover, even if the NaCl concentration increased to 2.0 mol/L, F0/F remained basically unchanged (Fig. 5C and Fig. S3-B), indicating N,S-CDs were hardly affected by ionic strength and can be used in complex environments. Long-term stability was also assessed, the PL intensity only dropped by 15% after storage at 4 °C for 60 days (Fig. S4). The results presented above reveal that the synthesized N,S-CDs have superior fluorescence stability, the QY measured with quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as a reference is as high as 36.8%, which is expected to serve as a fluorescent probe for practical detection.

According to the results described earlier, the N,S-CDs contained –NH2, –OH and –SH functional groups, which on the one hand enhance the water solubility and fluorescence stability, and on the other hand achieve selective recognition of Ag+. We selected 15 kinds of metal ions (2 mM) to access the selectivity of N,S-CDs. Ag+ had a strong quenching ability to PL intensity, while the effect of other 14 metal ions was very weak, suggesting that N,S-CDs can selectively recognize Ag+ (Fig. 6 A). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity reached a plateau within 5 min, indicating that N,S-CDs can achieve a fast response to Ag+ (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption peaks of Ag+ had no obvious overlap with the fluorescence excitation/emission peaks of N,S-CDs, so there was no fluorescence resonance energy transfer and internal filtering effects (Fig. S6) [27]. The unique selectivity to Ag+ is explained by the complexation between Ag+ and the amino or sulfhydryl in the structure, which causes N,S-CDs to agglomerate, thereby reducing the PL intensity.

To study the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs for Ag+ detection, we added Ag+ with different concentrations to the N,S-CDs solution, [Ag+] in the final mixed solution was between 0 and 800 μM. As the increase of [Ag+], the PL intensity at 435 nm was successively weakened. When [Ag+] was 800 μM, the PL intensity dropped to 30% of the initial intensity (Fig. 6B). lg(F0/F) showed three consecutive linear relationships with [Ag+] (0.1–700 μM) (Fig. 6C): lg(F0/F) = 0.0061[Ag+] + 0.0046, R2 = 0.9956 (0.1–3.0 μM); lg(F0/F) = 0.0004[Ag+] + 0.0231, R2 = 0.9937 (3.0–250 μM); lg(F0/F) = 0.0008[Ag+] − 0.0808, R2 = 0.9963 (250–700 μM). In contrast to the other Ag+ fluorescent probes (Table 1 ), the carbon source, nitrogen source and sulfur source required for the synthesis of N,S-CDs are simple in structure and non-toxic. Moreover, the probe constructed by N,S-CDs displayed a wider detection interval and a lower detection limit (0.03 μM, 3σ), so it has broad application prospects in actual sample detection.

In the actual sample detection process, in addition to Ag+, the sample also contains various other metal ions. Therefore, we also explored the fluorescence quenching of Ag+ when coexisting with other ions. The results in Fig. 6D showed that other interfering ions had almost no influence on the quenching effect of Ag+, so we used the standard addition method to appraise the feasibility of the constructed fluorescent probe for detecting Ag+ in actual tap water. The results in Table 2 displayed that the relative standard deviation (RSD) did not exceed 2.6%, and the spike recovery rate of Ag+ was between 97.2% and 101.8%. Therefore, the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs is accurate and reliable, can be applied to the detection of actual samples.

N,S-CDs synthesized from ammonium citrate and sodium thiosulfate by hydrothermal method, showed high sensitivity, selectivity and stability for Ag+ detection. The excellent luminescence performance of N,S-CDs mainly depends on its unique structure. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed the effective doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements, which effectively improved the QY of N,S-CDs. Moreover, FTIR results showed the existence of –NH2, –OH, –SH and other functional groups, which not only guaranteed good hydrophilicity of N,S-CDs, but also provided active sites for binding to Ag+, greatly improving the selectivity and response speed of fluorescence detection. Ag+ could bind to the –NH2 and –SH in the structure, which caused N,S-CDs to agglomerate, increasing the non-radiative transition of electrons and reducing the PL intensity. Moreover, Ag+ might combine with sulfur to form insoluble Ag2S, which affected the surface electron transfer, leading to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs achieved the specific recognition and quantitative measurement of Ag+.

In this work, we successfully synthesized a dual-element composite doped carbon quantum dots through a simple and rapid hydrothermal process without further chemical modification. The morphology, structure and element content of N,S-CDs were analyzed by a variety of characterization methods. The synthesized N,S-CDs had a narrow particle diameter distribution, with an average of 3.25 nm, and nitrogen and sulfur were effectively doped during the synthesis process. Fluorescence spectra analysis revealed that N,S-CDs showed high QY (36.8%), and exhibited significant selectivity in the construction of fluorescent probes to detect Ag+. Ag+ could combine with amino and sulfhydryl of N,S-CDs through Ag-N and Ag-S bonds, resulting in aggregation of N,S-CDs and fluorescence quenching. The constructed probe had a wide detection interval, and was not affected by other interferences, had remarkable reproducibility and stability. The fluorescent probe constructed by N,S-CDs exhibits outstanding detection performance, and has broad application prospects in environmental detection and biosensing.

Wenya Dong: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jiaqi Yan: Software, Data curation. Qingsheng Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaojun Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFC1808802), the Young and Middle-aged Science and Technology Talent Development Fund (ZQ2020-3) and the Talent Introduction Fund (YJ2021-33) of Shanghai Institute of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.109052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
